nativeRange.habitat: The rocky littoral zone of northern Lake Tanganyika, where it associates closely with rock surfaces to graze aufwuchs (the thin layer of algae and micro-organisms that coats submerged rocks). Found primarily in shallow to mid-depth rocky habitat, from about 1 to 30 metres.

decorPreferences: large piles of rockwork with many caves and crevices
open sandy areas between rock structures for territory delineation
minimal or no planting (disturbed by fish activity)
robust attached plants such as Anubias if used

diet.notes: In the wild, this species feeds almost exclusively on aufwuchs — the thin film of algae and micro-organisms that coats submerged rocks. In the aquarium, spirulina-based flakes and pellets should form the core of the diet, supplemented with blanched spinach, nori (dried seaweed), and other vegetable matter. Protein-rich foods such as beefheart, bloodworm, and high-protein pellets must be strictly avoided: the species has a long, specialised digestive tract adapted to low-protein plant material, and animal protein is a primary trigger for Tanganyikan bloat, which progresses rapidly and is often fatal. Small, frequent feedings of plant-based foods are strongly preferred over large infrequent meals.

goodWith: Synodontis petricola and other Tanganyikan Synodontis catfish
Altolamprologus compressiceps (with caution, in very large tanks)
Julidochromis species (with caution, in very large tanks)
avoidWith: other Tropheus species or morphs (cross-morph mixing causes hybridisation; aggression surges with mixed groups)
Malawi cichlids or other non-Tanganyikan cichlids
small or slow-moving fish that cannot escape aggression
any fish that will compete for algae-grazing territory
fish requiring soft or acidic water

imageCandidates.notes: Alexander Langer; 800x600. Group of adults showing the mature diagnostic look (blue-black body with vivid yellow lateral band) and colony behaviour of this shoaling cichlid; sharp, main fish fully in frame.
Mattia Nocciola; 3468x2317. Tack-sharp high-res live adult with vivid yellow band and blue face, whole fish and fins in frame; strongest single-subject detail shot.
lienyuan lee; 4221x2814. Iconic juvenile phase (jet-black body with white polka-dot spots) that gives the species its 'White-Spotted Tropheus' name; sharp, high-res, whole fish.

summary: An advanced-level Lake Tanganyika cichlid known for a striking colour change — jet-black with white spots as juveniles, maturing into a blue-black body with a vivid yellow or white lateral band. Strictly herbivorous and highly susceptible to Tanganyikan bloat if fed animal protein. Best kept in colonies of 15 or more to spread intraspecific aggression.
careNotes:
Tropheus duboisi is endemic to the northern rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika and is widely considered one of the most challenging Rift Lake cichlids to keep successfully. It is not a fish for beginners. Three factors combine to make it demanding: extreme dietary sensitivity, high intraspecific aggression, and rigid water-quality requirements.

Water quality must be pristine and stable. Hard, alkaline conditions mirroring Lake Tanganyika — pH 8.0–9.0, gH 10–25, kH 6–15 — are non-negotiable. Temperature should be maintained at 23–27°C. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero; nitrate should be kept below 20 ppm. A high-capacity filter and regular water changes of 20–30% per week are essential.

Diet is the single biggest cause of loss in captivity. The digestive system is highly adapted to low-protein aufwuchs grazing and cannot handle animal protein safely. Spirulina flakes (high spirulina content, ideally 30% or more), nori, blanched spinach, and other vegetable matter should make up the entire diet. Beefheart, bloodworms, and high-protein pellets must never be offered — even a single feeding can trigger Tanganyikan bloat (an internal bacterial infection or digestive blockage) that progresses rapidly and is often fatal. Small, frequent feedings two to three times daily are strongly preferred over large infrequent meals.

Intraspecific aggression is intense, particularly among males. Counterintuitively, larger groups of 15 to 20 or more actually reduce conflict by spreading it across many individuals — small groups of five or six commonly fight until only one remains. Aim for a ratio of roughly six to ten females per male. The tank footprint should be at least 150 cm to allow multiple territories to coexist. A species-only or near-species-only setup is strongly recommended; any tankmates should be restricted to non-competing Tanganyikan species such as Synodontis catfish.

Juveniles are sold in the iconic black-with-white-polka-dot pattern, which gradually transitions to the adult coloration — a blue-black body with a yellow or white lateral band — by around six to twelve months. Multiple geographical colour morphs exist, including Maswa, Kigoma, and Bemba; morphs must never be mixed, as doing so causes hybridisation and sharply increases intraspecific aggression. See also: [tropheus-moorii], the closely related sister species that shares the same husbandry demands.
breedingNotes:
Tropheus duboisi is a maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning takes place in open water over rocks in the typical Tanganyikan fashion, after which the female collects five to fifteen eggs and broods them orally for approximately four weeks. Fry are relatively large on release and can accept brine shrimp nauplii, microworms, and powdered spirulina foods immediately. The female continues limited brood care for a short period after first release, with fry returning to her mouth when threatened.

Breeding is difficult to achieve reliably. The primary challenge is male harassment: males seek to spawn constantly and will persistently pursue females, exhausting or killing them in smaller setups. A large colony with many females per male, abundant rockwork cover, and a minimum tank of 300 litres is needed to reduce harassment to manageable levels. Many keepers artificially strip fry from the female’s mouth at the two-week stage and raise them in a separate rearing tank to maximise yield and protect the female. Brood sizes are small — five to fifteen fry — making breeding less productive than with most aquarium cichlids.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tropheus duboisi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (12 cm), temperature (23–27°C), pH (8.0–9.5), hardness (8–25°H), minimum tank dimensions (60″ × 18″ × 18″ for groups of 15–20), diet (herbivore, no animal protein), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 5–15 eggs, ~4 weeks), grouping requirements (15–20 minimum), conspecific aggression pattern, juvenile colour phase, and bloat susceptibility.

additional.name: FishBase — Tropheus duboisi
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (12.0 cm TL), temperature (24–26°C), pH (8.5–9.0), hardness (dH 10–12), native distribution (northern Lake Tanganyika; DRC, Burundi, Tanzania), depth (to 30 m), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet (herbivorous aufwuchs grazer), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, female guards fry briefly after release), IUCN status (Endangered EN, B2ab(iii), assessed March 2025), minimum aquarium length (150 cm).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Tropheus: home is where the rocks are
additional.notes: Feature article on Tropheus genus husbandry. Confirmed colony-keeping requirements (10+ minimum, large species tank), male harassment as primary breeding challenge, Tanganyikan bloat risk from protein feeding, spirulina-based diet as standard of care, and artificial stripping technique for raising fry.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Tropheus Species Profiles
additional.notes: Additional hobbyist care reference. Confirmed pH 7.8–9.0, temperature 24–28°C, minimum 75 gal (284 L) for single specimen, 1.5 m+ tank length for colonies, dGH 10–15, kH 6+, nitrate below 20 ppm. Notes on robust plants (Anubias, Java fern), multiple geographical morphs, and strict no-mixing-of-morphs guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder that carries five to fifteen eggs for approximately four weeks. Male harassment is the primary challenge, requiring a large colony of 15 or more fish with a high female-to-male ratio and abundant rockwork cover. Artificial stripping at the two-week stage is common practice to protect females and improve fry yield. Fry are relatively large on release and accept brine shrimp nauplii and powdered spirulina immediately.
